data.title: Oculis upsized loan facility to access up to CHF 100 million
data.description:
• Oculis Holding (NASDAQ:OCS) has amended its loan facility with funds and accounts managed by BlackRock.
  • The upsized structure will provide CHF 75.0 million in borrowing capacity, which may be increased to up to CHF 100.0 million.
  • The loan comprises three tranches of CHF 25.0 million each, as well as an additional loan of up to CHF 25.0 million, which may be made available on mutually agreed terms.
  • No amounts were drawn at signing.

data.title: Myers Industries signals strategic shift with MTS review and targets $20M in cost savings by year-end 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Myers Industries (MYE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Aaron M. Schapper, President and CEO, stated that "second quarter review was lower year-over-year. We achieved strong growth in certain applications, notably in industrial, where demand for our military products remain healthy. However, we did encounter demand headwinds in most other end markets, primarily in vehicle and automotive aftermarket, resulting in lower sales across both segments." Schapper emphasized a positive outlook for the second half of the year, citing a "substantial backlog, primarily in industrial markets, especially military as well as infrastructure projects."
  • Schapper announced three key actions under the Focused Transformation program: a strategic review of the Myers Tire Supply (MTS) business, consolidation of rotational molding production capacity by idling two of nine facilities (projected annual savings of at least $3 million), and reaffirmation of the $20 million SG&A cost reduction target, noting "we have line of sight to $18 million, well on our way to our $20 million goal."
  • Schapper further explained, "with today's announcements, we have made a large significant step to simplify and focus our portfolio with the MTS strategic review. We are also rationalizing our operations with the consolidation of rotational molding production."
  • Daniel W. Hoehn, Interim CFO, reported, "Second quarter net sales were $209.6 million, down 4.8% from last year. Revenue was lower in both segments...The adjusted gross margin fell 220 basis points to 33.9% due to the lower volume, product sales mix and lower pricing, primarily in the Distribution segment."
  • Hoehn highlighted, "we reduced adjusted SG&A expenses 5%, keeping them essentially flat as a percentage of sales as we are beginning to see results from our Focused Transformation initiatives."

Outlook

  • The company reconfirmed its 2025 market outlook, maintaining expectations set in the previous quarter. Hoehn stated, "we still see both risks and opportunities for the businesses, and we'll continue to monitor end market conditions for impacts from tariffs or other factors that may influence demand trends."
  • Myers now expects "sales of our military products to exceed $40 million for the full year of 2025."
  • The industrial market is projected for moderate growth, infrastructure spending is expected to remain strong, and the vehicle end market is forecasted to be down due to economic uncertainty and tariff impacts.
  • Automotive aftermarket distribution is expected to be slightly down, with stabilization efforts ongoing through cost structure improvements and digital initiatives.

Financial Results

  • Net sales for the quarter were $209.6 million.
  • Adjusted gross margin was reported at 33.9%.
  • Adjusted operating income reached $22.8 million, with a margin of 10.9% of sales.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $24.7 million, up $22.7 million sequentially and $14.8 million from the prior year.
  • At quarter end, the company reported $239.7 million in revolving credit facility availability and $41.3 million in cash on hand.
  • Debt was reduced by $13 million during the quarter, with a reported net leverage ratio of 2.8x.
  • Share repurchases totaled $0.5 million in Q2, with $8.5 million remaining under the current authorization.

Q&A

  • Christian Zyla, KeyCorp: Asked about the rationale behind the MTS strategic review and the timeline that led to the decision. Schapper replied, "the MTS piece has been -- it's been an internal topic of discussion for some time...the first 6 months was really just to take the proper time to do the evaluation."
  • Zyla inquired about the size and relevance of the backlog. Hoehn responded, "for that business and as we grow in military, we'll see more backlog in those specific areas. I think for the other businesses, it depends, and we see a lot more kind of book and bill as we go."
  • Zyla also questioned the strength of free cash flow. Hoehn attributed it to timing and indicated that "historically, we've had a little bit more cash flow in the back half," and that recent performance reflects what the business should achieve.
  • Carolina Jolly, Gabelli & Company: Asked about confidence in a rebound for seed boxes and integration of the Signature acquisition. Schapper cited customer demand and seasonality for seed boxes and noted that Signature "brings a unique set of operational talents that we're actually able to really utilize across more of our material handling footprint."
  • William Dezellem, Tieton Capital Management: Asked about the impact of tariffs and idling of rotational molding plants. Hoehn explained export customers delayed purchases due to tariff uncertainty and Schapper added, "we do have some resolution, so we hope to not have those kind of disconnects in the next quarter."
  • Dezellem also probed on the fate of idled plants. Schapper clarified, "right now, that capacity is not needed," and both are leased facilities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with repeated focus on backlog visibility and strategic changes. The tone was positive when discussing transformation steps, but probing around demand, tariffs, and operational changes suggested underlying concern.
  • Management's prepared remarks were confident, as Schapper stated being "pleased with the progress we are making against our Focused Transformation objectives and confident in our team's ability to deliver improved financial results."
  • During Q&A, management maintained a steady but careful tone, occasionally referencing hope for future stability, as when Schapper said, "we hope to not have those kind of disconnects in the next quarter."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive regarding transformation actions, while analysts' skepticism persisted around execution and market challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q2 call introduced decisive strategic actions: the MTS business review and consolidation of manufacturing facilities, which were not present in Q1.
  • Cost reduction targets were reaffirmed and progress quantified, advancing from early-stage plans discussed last quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on the impact of tariffs, free cash flow, and market demand, but Q2 saw increased attention on backlog and the tangible progress of transformation.
  • Financial performance showed a sequential increase in free cash flow and continued cost discipline, while revenue declined year-over-year in Q2 versus flat performance in Q1.
  • Management's tone moved from establishing transformation plans to executing specific portfolio and operational changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Demand headwinds in vehicle and automotive aftermarket segments remain a challenge.
  • Tariff uncertainty continues to affect timing and volume of export sales, though management anticipates some near-term resolution.
  • The strategic review of MTS introduces uncertainty regarding the future of that business and its impact on the overall portfolio.
  • Two rotational molding plants are being idled, reflecting lower current demand; management stated the capacity could be revisited if needed in the future.

Final Takeaway

Myers Industries is accelerating its Focused Transformation strategy with a strategic review of its Myers Tire Supply business and operational consolidation, targeting $20 million in cost reductions by the end of 2025. Despite near-term demand softness in several markets and ongoing tariff uncertainty, the company is maintaining a positive outlook for the second half of the year, supported by a strong backlog in military and infrastructure applications. Management underscores confidence in achieving financial targets and further portfolio simplification, while continuing to focus on disciplined capital allocation and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quanta Services raises 2025 outlook to $27.4B–$27.9B revenue and $10.28–$10.88 EPS while advancing strategic acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quanta Services (PWR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Earl C. Austin, President and CEO, reported "strong double-digit growth in revenue, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted earnings per share, along with record backlog of $35.8 billion and a number of other record financial metrics." The company announced the acquisition of Dynamic Systems, described as "a premier turnkey mechanical, plumbing and process infrastructure solutions provider" that will expand Quanta's addressable market and capabilities, particularly in technology, manufacturing, and other load center markets. Austin stated that Dynamic Systems "brings an exceptional management team and a premier craft-skilled workforce that complement Quanta's culture."
  • Austin noted a strategic investment in Bell Lumber and Pole Company, enhancing Quanta's core utility infrastructure equipment portfolio. He emphasized that "Quanta's core strategy is built on the foundation of craft-skilled labor, execution certainty, investment discipline and clear strategic rationale."
  • Austin highlighted the increasing demand for power and infrastructure: "Utilities across the United States are experiencing and forecasting meaningful increases in power demand, which is being driven by the adoption of new technologies and related infrastructure, including data centers and artificial intelligence..."
  • CFO Jayshree S. Desai stated, "This morning, we reported strong second quarter results, including revenues of $6.8 billion, net income attributable to common stock of $229 million or $1.52 per diluted share, adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.48 and adjusted EBITDA of $669 million." She reported cash flow from operations of $296 million and free cash flow of $170 million. Desai added, "Our second quarter performance was ahead of our expectations across most financial metrics and similar to the first quarter, is allowing us to increase our full year expectations before considering the contribution from acquisitions."
  • Desai explained, "We are currently evaluating refinancing alternatives to increase our post-transaction liquidity profile, ensuring we can continue to support operations and opportunistically invest capital."

Outlook

  • Desai raised full-year 2025 guidance: "Revenues are now expected to range between $27.4 billion and $27.9 billion, adjusted EBITDA between $2.76 billion and $2.89 billion and adjusted EPS between $10.28 and $10.88."
  • She noted confidence in demand: "While we recognize the variability in the regulatory environment, demand for Quanta's differentiated portfolio of self-performed craft labor solutions remains strong."
  • The increased 2025 financial expectations were attributed to solid second quarter performance and expected contributions from recent acquisitions and investments.

Financial Results

  • The company reported revenues of $6.8 billion, net income attributable to common stock of $229 million, $1.52 per diluted share, adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.48, and adjusted EBITDA of $669 million.
  • Cash flow from operations reached $296 million, and free cash flow was $170 million for the quarter.
  • Quanta achieved a record backlog of $35.8 billion.
  • Management stated that second quarter performance was "ahead of our expectations across most financial metrics."

Q&A

  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citi Research, asked about sequential backlog growth and long-term EPS growth potential despite political and regulatory noise. Austin responded, "I do believe the things that we're doing today set us up for the future, '26, '27, '28 and '29. What we've done is, we basically built platforms against TAMs that compound."
  • Sherif El-Sabbahy, Bank of America, inquired about backlog and bidding process. Austin explained, "It's longer in nature. We're talking about '26, '27, '28 type time frames of growth and CAGR growth and EPS growth and EBITDA growth."
  • Atidrip Modak, Goldman Sachs, focused on the Dynamic Systems acquisition. Austin said the acquisition was customer-driven and aligned with Quanta's strategy, adding, "Yes, we will continue to look at great family businesses that add value to our strategy and our shareholders."
  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital, questioned the outlook for renewables given policy changes. Austin replied, "I still believe, it's really about the lowest cost, the speed to market that the renewables provide, we are seeing pull-ins on LNTPs."
  • Jamie Cook, Truist Securities, asked about internal preparations for short-term slowdowns and revenue synergies from Dynamic. Austin described labor flexibility and portfolio derisking: "We do move labor quite a bit across segments across TAMs."
  • Jayshree Desai confirmed Dynamic Systems had about $1.8 billion of backlog, which would be updated in Q3.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth sustainability, strategic acquisitions, labor flexibility, and regulatory risks. The tone was largely constructive with some pressing for detail on renewables, backlog, and M&A rationale.
  • Management maintained a confident, forward-looking tone, emphasizing strong demand, portfolio resilience, and the strategic value of recent acquisitions. Austin stated, "We intend to remain investment grade."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident, with even more emphasis on growth opportunities from acquisitions and increasing addressable markets. Analyst sentiment was similarly constructive but included more questions about the impact and integration of new acquisitions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS was raised again this quarter, following a similar upward revision in Q1, but now includes the expected contribution from Dynamic Systems and Bell Lumber.
  • Management placed greater emphasis on platform acquisitions and cross-segment labor flexibility this quarter, describing Dynamic Systems as a new growth platform.
  • Record backlog increased from $35.3 billion in Q1 to $35.8 billion in Q2.
  • Analysts' questions shifted toward integration of recent acquisitions and the evolving renewables landscape, compared to Q1's focus on core market growth and transmission opportunities.
  • Management continued to stress portfolio derisking and long-term growth drivers, with explicit references to multi-year growth outlooks extending through 2029.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged variability in the regulatory environment and the potential impact of policy changes on renewables but expressed confidence in customer safe harbor strategies and multiyear pipelines.
  • Analysts raised concerns about policy headwinds, safe harbor rules, and the pace of renewables, but management indicated that "demand side from every one of our customers we keep hearing is as strong as ever."
  • The company is monitoring liquidity and leverage post-acquisition, with Desai stating that Quanta aims to maintain leverage between 1.5x and 2x and expects to be below 2x by year-end.

Final Takeaway

Quanta Services management highlighted strong financial performance, record backlog, and the successful integration of strategic acquisitions such as Dynamic Systems and Bell Lumber as key drivers of increased 2025 guidance. The company emphasized its platform approach, labor flexibility, and solution-based model, positioning itself for sustained long-term growth across expanding addressable markets, while maintaining a prudent balance sheet and active risk management amid regulatory and policy uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil States outlines $88M–$93M EBITDA guidance and margin growth as offshore mix rises
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oil States International (OIS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Cynthia B. Taylor, President and CEO, highlighted the company's performance "achieving the midpoint of our guided EBITDA range for the second quarter of 2025 due to our product and service mix." She reported that "72% of our consolidated revenues were generated from offshore and international projects, up significantly sequentially and year-over-year." Taylor described a strategic shift toward international project-driven revenues and ongoing optimization of U.S. land operations amid lower domestic activity and competitive market dynamics.
  • Taylor stated, "Driven by strong demand across our international and offshore markets, our Offshore/Manufactured Products segment delivered strong performance. Revenues increased 15% sequentially, while adjusted segment EBITDA rose 18%. Backlog increased to $363 million again, allowing us to achieve our highest level since September 2015."
  • The company exited three additional land-based facilities and reduced its U.S. land-focused workforce in response to declining U.S. land drilling and completion activity. Taylor emphasized, "Our deleveraging efforts should unlock additional equity value to our stockholders as we have approached net debt 0 and pay off our convertible senior notes at their maturity in April 2026."
  • Lloyd A. Hajdik, Executive VP and CFO, stated, "During the second quarter, we generated revenues of $165 million and adjusted consolidated EBITDA of $21 million. Net income totaled $3 million or $0.05 per share, which included facility exit, severance and other charges and credits totaling $3 million. Our adjusted net income totaled $5 million or $0.09 per share after excluding these charges and credits."

Outlook

  • Taylor confirmed, "We are maintaining our full year EBITDA guidance in a range between $88 million to $93 million. However, our revenue guidance needs to be updated for the streamlining of our U.S. land operations, which will reduce our full year revenue range to $685 million to $700 million."
  • For the third quarter, Taylor provided guidance for "revenues in the range of $165 million to $170 million and EBITDA of $21 million to $23 million."
  • The company expects "cash flow from operations, which are still expected to be in a range of $65 million to $75 million for the full year."

Financial Results

  • Hajdik reported, "Our Offshore/Manufactured Products segment generated revenues of $107 million and adjusted segment EBITDA of $21 million in the second quarter. Adjusted segment EBITDA margin was 20% in the second quarter compared to 19% in the first quarter."
  • The Completion and Production Services segment delivered "revenues of $29 million and adjusted segment EBITDA of $8 million in the second quarter. Adjusted segment EBITDA margin was 28%, benefiting from facility and equipment sale gains in the second quarter compared to 25% in the first quarter."
  • Downhole Technologies segment produced "revenues of $29 million and $1 million of adjusted segment EBITDA in the second quarter."
  • The company generated $15 million of cash flow from operations, used $10 million for CapEx, received $3 million from asset sales, repurchased $7 million in common stock, and purchased $15 million of convertible senior notes.

Q&A

  • James Michael Rollyson, Raymond James: Asked for color on offshore project timing and the impact of uncertainty. Taylor responded that project deferrals seen elsewhere are "discretionary type investments" and that Oil States' business is "much more weighted to production infrastructure associated with these large fields that have already been drilled and discovered," emphasizing multi-decade developments and robust bookings outlook.
  • Rollyson asked about tariff impacts. Taylor said, "we just don't anticipate a material impact from the tariff situation, given our variety of global supply sourcing... The one area that we'll probably have modest cost increases is actually in the Downhole...the perforating side of the business, which, as you know, is rather small for us."
  • Rollyson asked for CapEx guidance clarity. Hajdik answered, "we're going to guide to CapEx about $30 million because we are a little higher in the second quarter for the completion of the Batam and some specific built riser equipment for customer contracts." Taylor added, "what's new is this low-impact workover riser...equipment built for future revenue streams."
  • Patrick John Ouellette, Stifel: Queried normalized revenue mix. Taylor stated, "of the 28% current land base mix, about half of that comes from our Downhole Technologies segment."
  • Ouellette asked about margin progression. Hajdik projected, "Upper 20s to low 30s." Taylor said, "noticeable almost a doubling of our EBITDA margins by these actions."
  • John Matthew Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about U.S. land-based business mix. Taylor said, "everything we have left is really focused on completion activity, 0 drilling."
  • Charles P. Minervino, Susquehanna: Questioned full year guidance step-up. Taylor explained, "It is going to be led by our Offshore/Manufactured Products Segment. And most of it is based on backlog build."
  • Stephen David Gengaro, Stifel: Asked about offshore order flow. Taylor responded, "we are looking at a book-to-bill north of 1 throughout the balance of this year and do have optimism as we go into 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts probed guidance sustainability, margin improvements, and the competitive landscape, with a neutral-to-positive tone, especially as Taylor and Hajdik addressed concerns with specifics and confidence.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing actions such as "our bookings outlook remains robust," "we anticipate continued strength," and "we are committed to optimizing our operations." Taylor's language was assertive regarding backlog, cost structure, and future outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sustained a constructive, confident, and well-articulated exchange, with the main shift being more emphasis on offshore and international strengths and clarity regarding U.S. land exit impacts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reaffirmed full-year EBITDA guidance but lowered revenue guidance (from $700 million–$735 million to $685 million–$700 million) due to further U.S. land operation streamlining.
  • Margin outlook improved, with a focus on higher offshore/international mix and cost reductions, compared to the previous quarter's emphasis on broad-based diversification and initial land optimization.
  • Backlog hit a new decade high, and the share of offshore/international revenues rose from 1Q's strong levels to 72% in 2Q.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from cost and tariff impacts to sustainability of offshore momentum and the effects of U.S. land retrenchment on long-term margins.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing geopolitical instability, lower crude oil prices, and fluctuating U.S. trade policies as macro risks.
  • Taylor noted, "U.S. land drilling and completion activity declined significantly during the period," and that further rationalization may continue.
  • Tariff-related cost increases are expected to be modest and concentrated in the Downhole Technologies segment.
  • Management's mitigation measures include further facility exits, workforce reductions, and global sourcing flexibility.

Final Takeaway

Oil States International emphasized its strategic pivot toward offshore and international markets, delivering strong backlog growth and improved margins, while continuing to optimize its cost structure and streamline U.S. land operations. The company maintained its full-year EBITDA outlook but adjusted revenue guidance to reflect business mix changes. Management remained confident in sustained cash flow generation, future bookings, and the ability to unlock equity value through disciplined capital allocation, all underpinned by a robust offshore project pipeline and ongoing deleveraging efforts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: AMZN, FIG, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged down Friday morning as investor sentiment took a hit from concerns over the economic impact of higher tariffs, with market participants also awaiting the closely watched U.S. payrolls report due later in the day.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Figma (NYSE:FIG) +18% – Shares extended gains after a stunning NYSE debut on Thursday, where they soared 250% intraday, briefly topping $105. The design software firm priced its IPO at $33 per share, above the expected range, and saw its market value surpass $50B shortly after listing. The explosive rally marked one of the most dramatic IPO openings in recent memory.

  • Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) +14% – Shares rallied after the company delivered a double beat in Q2 and issued strong Q3 guidance. Daily active users rose 21% Y/Y to 110.4M, while revenue surged 78% to $500M, driven by 84% growth in ad sales. Q3 revenue is expected at $535M–$545M above the consensus of $472.7M with adjusted EBITDA of $185M–$195M (consensus $159.5M).

Biggest stock losers

  • Coinbase Global (NASDAQ:COIN) -9% – Shares plunged after Q2 adjusted EPS of $0.12 fell far short of the $1.49 consensus, sharply down from $1.93 in the prior quarter and $1.10 a year ago. Total net revenue of $1.42B also missed estimates of $1.59B, declining from $1.96B in Q1 but up from $1.38B a year earlier. Looking ahead, Coinbase expects Q3 subscription and services revenue of $665M–$745M (midpoint $705M vs. $742M consensus), transaction expenses to grow in the mid-teens as a percentage of net revenue, tech and G&A costs of $800M–$850M, and sales and marketing spend of $190M–$290M.

  • Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) -7% – Shares declined even after the company topped Q2 expectations on revenue, EPS, and operating income, as its Q3 operating income guidance midpoint came in below consensus. Amazon expects Q3 revenue of $174.0B–$179.5B (midpoint $176.3B vs. $173.3B consensus) and operating income of $15.5B–$20.5B (midpoint $18.0B vs. $19.4B consensus). CEO Andy Jassy highlighted constraints in AI growth due to limited electricity and chip supply, noting capital expenditures of $31.4 billion in Q2 will likely persist for the remainder of the year as Amazon invests further in chips, data centers, and power infrastructure.

data.title: Li Auto’s July deliveries slip nearly 40% Y/Y
data.description:
• Li Auto (NASDAQ:LI) delivered 30,731 vehicles in July 2025, compared to 36,279 vehicles delivered in June 2025.
  • That marks a 15.29% month-over-month drop and a 39.74% year-on-year in deliveries. In the first seven months of the year, Li Auto delivered 234,669 vehicles, a year-on-year decrease of 2.21%.
  • Peer, Xpeng (XPEV) set a new monthly record in July with 36,717 Smart EV deliveries, a 229% year-over-year surge. It’s the ninth straight month the company has exceeded 30,000 deliveries.

data.title: Eurozone annual inflation rate unchanged at 2% in July
data.description:
• The inflation rate in the Euro Area remained unchanged at 2% in July.

  • The annual core inflation rate in the Eurozone, which excludes prices for energy, food, alcohol & tobacco, steadied at 2.3% in July 2025, the same as in each of the previous two months, and compared to forecasts of 2.2%, preliminary estimates showed.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (DAX)

Currency: (EUR:USD)

data.title: Magna International beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; updates FY25 outlook

data.title: Zeekr Group delivers 44K cars in July, up 20% Y/Y
data.description:
• Zeekr Group (NYSE:ZK) delivered a total of 44,193 vehicles in July across its Zeekr and Lynk & Co brands, representing increases of 19.7% year-over-year and 2.7% month-over-month. 
  • Specifically, the Zeekr brand delivered 16,977 vehicles, while Lynk & Co delivered 27,216 vehicles.
